region_of_origin: Native to Central and South America; widely cultivated across Asia including Singapore, Malaysia, China, and Japan
food_subgroup: Root vegetables and tubers — starchy. Orange-fleshed variety of Ipomoea batatas, high in beta-carotene.

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for raw orange-flesh sweet potato as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for individual raw vegetables is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium raw peeled orange-flesh sweet potato weighs approximately 130g and provides around 112 kcal, 26g carbohydrate, and over 900mcg RAE of Vitamin A.

nutrient_source.title: Sweet Potato, raw, unprepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168482); cross-referenced with Health Promotion Board Singapore Foodo database
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service SR Legacy + Health Promotion Board Singapore (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 168482 is raw sweet potato, unprepared — directly matches this record for raw peeled orange-flesh sweet potato. Values cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo for regional validation. All nutrient values confirmed consistent between sources.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sweet Potato (boiled) — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~44 (low) for boiled sweet potato per University of Sydney GI Database. This record represents raw peeled sweet potato; GI value applies to the boiled preparation which is the standard reference state. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (44 × 20.1) / 100 = 8.8 per 100g serving. Note: baked sweet potato has a significantly higher GI of approximately 61–75 (medium to medium-high) due to greater starch gelatinisation during baking.

hypertension.key_factor: High potassium (337mg/100g), moderate natural sodium (55mg/100g), magnesium (25mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Orange-flesh sweet potato is beneficial for hypertension management: its high potassium content (337mg/100g) supports sodium-potassium balance and vasodilation, while its naturally moderate sodium (55mg/100g, not added salt) and magnesium (25mg/100g) further support blood pressure regulation — consume boiled or steamed without added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI is cooking-method dependent: boiled GI ~44 (low, GL ~8.8/100g); baked GI ~61–75 (medium-high, GL ~13–15/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled or steamed orange-flesh sweet potato (GI ~44, GL ~8.8 per 100g) is a suitable carbohydrate choice for Type 2 Diabetes management, but patients must be counselled that baking or roasting raises the GI to approximately 61–75 — negating the glycemic advantage — and that the carbohydrate content (~20g/100g) requires portion control comparable to other starchy foods.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0g), high beta-carotene/Vitamin A (709mcg RAE/100g), potassium (337mg/100g), dietary fibre (3g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Orange-flesh sweet potato is cardioprotective: it contains no saturated or trans fat, provides 3g dietary fibre per 100g to support LDL reduction, and delivers 709mcg RAE beta-carotene (a potent antioxidant) and 337mg potassium per 100g — all consistent with a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High beta-carotene/Vitamin A (709mcg RAE/100g), Vitamin B6 (0.29mg/100g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The exceptionally high beta-carotene content (709mcg RAE Vitamin A per 100g) provides antioxidant activity that may help scavenge free radicals associated with synovial inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis; Vitamin B6 (0.29mg/100g) additionally supports immune modulation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sweet potato (orange-flesh variety, Ipomoea batatas) — known as 番薯 (fān shǔ) in Chinese, keledek in Malay, and satsumaimo (サツマイモ) in Japanese — is a starchy root vegetable widely consumed across Singapore, Malaysia, China, Japan, and Southeast Asia. The orange flesh is caused by high beta-carotene content, which is converted to Vitamin A in the body; at 709mcg RAE per 100g, a single 130g medium sweet potato provides well over the adult daily Vitamin A requirement. Raw peeled sweet potato has a mildly sweet flavour and firm, dense texture; it softens significantly when boiled or steamed. Per 100g raw peeled: 86 kcal, 20.1g carbohydrate, 1.6g protein, 0.1g fat, 3g dietary fibre, 337mg potassium, 55mg sodium, 77.3g water. The glycemic index is critically cooking-method dependent: boiled or steamed GI ~44 (low, GL ~8.8/100g) versus baked or roasted GI ~61–75 (medium to medium-high, GL ~13–15/100g). This distinction is clinically important for Type 2 Diabetes counselling in Singapore, where sweet potato is frequently recommended as a 'healthier rice substitute' — patients who bake rather than boil may not achieve the expected glycemic benefit. In Singapore cuisine, orange sweet potato appears in dessert soups (tang shui 糖水), pumpkin-sweet potato soup, Malay kueh (e.g. kueh bengkang), and increasingly as a boiled side vegetable or T2DM rice substitute. Available year-round at all wet markets and supermarkets. Note: 'yam' in Singapore colloquial usage sometimes refers to sweet potato (particularly in older Hokkien/Teochew communities) but botanically refers to taro (Colocasia esculenta) — these are distinct foods with different nutritional profiles.
common_user_queries: orange sweet potato vs white sweet potato
is sweet potato good for diabetes

yam: NOTE: yam and sweet potato are different plants — 'yam' in Singapore sometimes refers to taro (Colocasia esculenta) or to sweet potato depending on community usage

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 168482 (raw sweet potato, unprepared) — an exact match — cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo. GI value from University of Sydney GI Database for boiled sweet potato. All core macros, secondary nutrients, vitamins, and minerals are fully populated from approved primary sources. Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2) are verified and defensible.
notes: cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero (0.0) — sweet potato is a plant food and naturally contains no cholesterol. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero (0.0) — sweet potato is a plant food and naturally contains no vitamin B12. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero (0.0) — sweet potato does not naturally contain vitamin D. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero (0.0) per USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 168482. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero (0.0) per USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 168482. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are reported as 0.0 based on USDA SR Legacy data showing negligible fatty acid fractions. water_g set to 77.3g per USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 168482 (raw sweet potato, unprepared). GI value of 44 applies to boiled sweet potato (the standard reference preparation); raw sweet potato GI is not directly measured. Baked sweet potato GI is approximately 61–75 (medium to medium-high). The sodium value of 55mg/100g is naturally occurring in the vegetable and is not added salt. Two portions are defined: 100g (reference) and 130g (1 medium sweet potato). Glycemic load per serving calculated at 100g reference serving: GL = (44 × 20.1) / 100 = 8.8. Japanese local name サツマイモ（オレンジ色の果肉、生、皮むき）added to satisfy G6 completeness for global food coverage; this food is not a Japanese-origin dish so Japanese is not a mandatory language per G6, but the name is included for alias completeness given the food's widespread use in Japan.
